coredata.dc:title: Novel synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from bio-based Carica Papaya Leaves: Optical and structural properties with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities
coredata.dc:creator: Gudimella, Krishna kanthi
Gedda, Gangaraju
Kumar, P. Senthil
Babu, B.K.
Yamajala, Bhaskar
Rao, Battula Venkateswara
Singh, Prabal Pratap
Kumar, Deepak
Sharma, Ajit
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are prepared from a natural resource Carica Papaya Leaves, by sand bath method. The as-synthesized CDs optical and structural properties were characterized by UltraViolet-Visible, FT- IR, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Also the size, shape, and particle size distribution was studied using Transmission electron microscopy technique. These CDs were examined for biomedical applications like free radical scavenging activity using DPPH assay, antioxidant activity using phosphomolybdate assay, and in vitro anti-inflammatory activity using membrane stabilization protocol. The CDs exhibited excellent biological activities at lower concentrations and showed notable half-maximal effective concentration (EC50). The EC50 of free radical scavenging activity (27.6 μg/mL), antioxidant activity (23.00 μg/mL), and in vitro anti-inflammatory activity (15.52 μg/mL) signifies that CDs can be potential therapeutic agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antioxidant
Anti-inflammatory
Free radical scavenging
Natural precursor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectral data of CDs (a) UV Visible spectra of CDs. (Inset: Fluorescence image) (b) Photoluminescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 250 nm to 650 nm with increments of 50 nm.
(a) FTIR spectrum of CDs (b) TEM image of CDs (Inset: Size distribution histogram of CDs observed from TEM image).

Free radical scavenging activity of CDs (a) UV visible absorption spectra of DPPH assay showing a gradual decrease in absorption intensity at 517 nm (b) Inhibition percentage of DPPH radical Vs. The concentration of CDs and ascorbic acid (c) Optical images showing color change from violet to pale yellow. d: EC50 (Free radical scavenging activity). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Total antioxidant activity of CDs by Phosphomolybdate assay (a) UV visible absorption spectra of TAC activity showing a gradual increase in absorption intensity at 695 nm (b) Total antioxidant capacity Vs. The concentration of CDs and ascorbic acid (c) Optical images showing a change in intensity from pale to deep bluish-green. d: EC50 (Total antioxidant activity). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Anti-inflammatory activity of CDs using HRBC membrane stabilization (a) UV visible absorption spectra of HRBC membrane stabilization, showing a gradual decrease in absorption intensity at 560 nm (b) Percentage of HRBC membrane protection Vs. the concentration of CDs and diclofenac(c) Optical images showing Haemoglobin content in the supernatant. d: EC50 (In vitro anti-inflammatory assay).
Schematic illustration of the sand bath assisted synthesis of CDs from Carica papaya leaves.
Natural precursors derived CDs for various applications.

row.entry: Imaging of Bacterial and fungal cells

head.ce:title: Novel synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from bio-based Carica Papaya Leaves: Optical and structural properties with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are prepared from a natural resource Carica Papaya Leaves, by sand bath method. The as-synthesized CDs optical and structural properties were characterized by UltraViolet-Visible, FT- IR, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Also the size, shape, and particle size distribution was studied using Transmission electron microscopy technique. These CDs were examined for biomedical applications like free radical scavenging activity using DPPH assay, antioxidant activity using phosphomolybdate assay, and in vitro anti-inflammatory activity using membrane stabilization protocol. The CDs exhibited excellent biological activities at lower concentrations and showed notable half-maximal effective concentration (EC50). The EC50 of free radical scavenging activity (27.6 μg/mL), antioxidant activity (23.00 μg/mL), and in vitro anti-inflammatory activity (15.52 μg/mL) signifies that CDs can be potential therapeutic agents.

ce:section.ce:para: The evolution and rise of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles took place in 2004 and 2006 thru the experiments led by X. Xu, 2004 and Sun et al., respectively. Typically, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with a diameter less than 10 nm are named carbon dots (CDs). Their efforts led to the exploration of CDs with low toxicity and high stability (Dong et al., 2014; Gudimella, 2021). Also, the physical and chemical properties (Y. Wang, 2017) enabled developing applications by overcoming the traditional drawbacks like usage of heavy metals, carcinogenic solvents, and high temperatures. Furthermore, CDs possess a strong ability to bind with organic and inorganic molecules easily due to their surface functional groups. Based on these advantages, developed a series of controllable synthetic techniques and explored various applications photocatalysis, chemical sensing, bioimaging, drug carrier, fluorescent markers, phototherapies, and many more. (Chiu et al., 2016; Hong et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2017; Yuan et al., 2016; Zhang and Yu, 2016). Researchers are currently focusing on highlighting the biomedical applications of CDs such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities to fill the gap caused by the lack of adequate progress and exploration.

Antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties are prominent in biomedical applications, as they are a root cause in developing disorders in biological living systems (O.I. Aruoma). The generation of free radicals is responsible for developing such disorders by causing deformation of proteins, damage to Deoxyribonucleic acid, cancer, etc. In addition to this, inflammation has become common in present-day life nowadays and interlinked with prolonged disorders by affecting the cell membranes. The traditional treatments are imposing several side effects on frequent usage like kidney failure, heart strokes etc. Thus, it indicates the vital and necessity of developing an alternate such as CDs, which can show a minor effect on living systems. This provoked to develop CDs and screened for antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents by combining traditional medicinal plant species and advanced nanoscience. Also, naturally derived CDs exhibit low toxicity when compared to metal CDs. Therefore, it shows significance in developing CDs as a potential candidate to exhibit biomedical applications.

Here the vital task is selecting precursors for CDs that are renewable and whose availability is also high. Carica Papaya is most available in maximum countries, and the plant belongs to the genus Carica of the family Caricaceae. The plant is known to possess vital nutrients and bioactive compounds namely antioxidants, vitamins and minerals which offers many health benefits (Santana et al., 2019). The plants' medicinal richness is widely used in herbal (Wangkheirakpam, 2018), natural curing like ayurvedic (Naik et al., 2020), and other drug formulations (Nafiu et al., 2019). All parts of the plant, i.e., bark, leaves, and fruits, were used in various disease treatments (Charan et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2020), including blood pressure, increasing the platelet count in blood, cancer, diabetes, and malaria, and dengue fever (Singh et al., 2020). The peel and fruits of papaya are rich in vitamins, phenols, proteolytic enzymes, which render them a good antioxidant and an outstanding antimicrobial agent (Asghar et al., 2016). Based on recent research reports, papaya fruit extract exerts antioxidant and immunostimulant properties against acrylamide toxicity in rats. Many reports focussed on exploring peels and fruit of Carica Papaya for biomedical applications. Literature studies gave no much information about the Carica Papaya leaves bio-applicability and industrial uses (Balakrishnan, 2015). Table 1 shows Carica Papaya leaves have shown better EC50 value when compared to precursors like cumin seeds, coconut shells, coriander leaves. Also to our knowledge, Total antioxidant capacity and anti-inflammatory activity were not so far reported for naturally derived precursors. Hence leaves were chosen as a precursor for synthesizing CDs and screened for respective applications like antioxidant and anti-inflammatory.

In the present work, we focused on using a simple, greener, cost-effective, and eco-friendly practice to fabricate fluorescent CDs from Carica papaya leaves. Also, an inexpensive sand bath approach that operates at mild temperatures was chosen to synthesize CDs in bulk quantities. In the synthesis of CDs, the usage of acids/bases or toxic chemicals was avoided. This study also demonstrates the innovative ‘green solution’ to dispose of ripped/fallen papaya leaves. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited outstanding potential for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities.

Ascorbic acid, ammonium molybdate, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), sodium phosphate, disodium orthophosphate, and sodium chloride were manufactured by Sisco Research Laboratories, India, procured from Anu Scientific, Hyderabad. Solvents like dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, concentrated sulphuric acid, and methanol were of Merck make and purchased from Hychem Laboratories. All the chemicals and solvents were of reagent grade with 99.9% purity and utilized for experiments as acquired without further processing. Demineralized water (DM) was used for preparations and analysis.

The photoluminescence of CDs was captured at 365 nm using handheld UV lamp Analytik Jena UVL-56, U.S.A. Absorption spectra were recorded on a double beam spectrophotometer, Shimhazdu 1800, Japan using Quartz cuvettes. The fluorescence spectrum was obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, RF 6000, Japan. Functional groups were analyzed by fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) on Bruker ATR-FTIR, U.S.A instrument. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were captured using HRTEM JEOL 2100, 200 kV. U.S.A. For analyzing the particle size and distribution in TEM images, Image J software was used. Origin-9 software was used to draw the Gaussian non-linear curve and all spectrums. All the experimentations were performed in triplicates (n = 3).

For the preparation of CDs, the Carica papaya leaves were washed thoroughly with normal water five times. Then, veins were removed carefully. After that, leaves are cut to 2 × 2 cm pieces approximately. 10 g of the leaf's pieces were blended in a domestic grinder by adding a minimal amount of DM water. The green solution was diluted with 50 mL DM water and filtered using a whatmann filter paper (nanopore size). The filtrate was transferred in a round-bottomed flask and mounted on a sand bath. The temperature is maintained at 180 οC for 24 h with continuous stirring. During the period, the green color solution turned to a brown color solution. Then the solution was left at room temperature for a while to reduce the temperature. After cooling, the solution was filtered using a membrane syringe (filter 0.22 μM) to remove insoluble particles. A clear yellowish-brown CDs solution obtained was stored at 4 οC for characterizations and biomedical applications. The concentration of the CDs is 25 μg/mL.

The free radical scavenging capacity of the CDs was determined by using the DPPH assay (Brand-Williams et al., 1995). Typically, 40 μL DMSO with various concentrations of CDs (0–50 μg/μL) was added to 3.95 mL of 0.1 mM methanolic DPPH. The reagent solution was kept in the dark for 30 min. The absorbance was recorded at 517 nm using DMSO as a control. The inhibition percentage and free radical scavenging capacity was obtained using the formulae.
I n h i b i t i o n
%
=
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l − A b s o r b a n c e
o f
s a m p l e
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
× 100

3.0 mL of the Phosphomolybdate reagent (reagent preparation is provided in supporting information 1.1) was added and mixed with CDs with varying concentrations (0–50 μg/μL). Similarly, 3.0 mL of the reagent was added to deionized water for positive control, and ascorbic acid was taken as reference. The samples were incubated for 90 min at 90οC and allowed to cool. Absorbance was recorded at 695 nm for the samples after cooling. DM water was used as a control.
T o t a l
a n t i o x i d a n t
c a p a c i t y
( T A C )
=
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l − A b s o r b a n c e
o f
s a m p l e
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protocol for preparing blood samples for Human Red Blood Cells membrane stabilization method
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro anti-inflammatory assay for CDs was performed following the Human Red Blood Cells (HRBC) (Vane and Botting, 1995) method based on the membrane stabilization principle. About 2.0 mL of the blood sample was collected from healthy volunteers aged below 35, who have not used any medication for atleast a period of two weeks. The sample was transferred to a heparinized purple capped EDTA tube, and uniformly shaken up and down to prevent coagulation. The test samples were centrifuged for 15 min at 3000 rotations per minute (RPM) to separate red blood cells (RBC) and plasma. The RBC was thoroughly washed with an isosaline (0.9% NaCl) solution several times and centrifuged. The centrifugate is diluted with isosaline to make a 10% v/v solution. The as-prepared solution is the HRBC suspension and used for analysis.

0.5 mL of the HRBC suspension was added to various concentrations of CDs (0–45 μg/μL) along with 2.0 mL of hypo saline (0.36%) and 1.0 mL of phosphate buffer (0.15 M, pH 7.4). DM Water was added in place of hyposaline for control. Diclofenac was used as a standard reference control. The analysis mixture was incubated at 37 οC for 30 min and centrifuged for 15 min at 3000 RPM. The supernatant was collected and analyzed at 560 nm spectrophotometrically. The absorption determines the percentage of haemoglobin in the supernatant.

The percentage of haemolysis was calculated using the following formula:
H e m o l y s i s
% =
(
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
s a m p l e
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
)
× 100
P e r c e n t a g e
o f
p r o t e c t i o n = 100 − H e m o l y s i s
%

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies and characteristic analysis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis CDs were prepared by the sand bath method using papaya leaves as a precursor. The leaves majorly constitute ascorbic acid, tocopherol, flavonoids, papain, cyanogenic glucosides, and glucosinolates (Nguyen et al., 2021). These compounds contain mainly nitrogen and oxygen elements along with carbon. Thus, papaya leaves act as a significant precursor and easily transform into CDs by dehydration, pyrolysis, and carbonization followed by dearomatization for CDs formation. The precursor's probable mechanism to transforming into CDs can be relayed as dehydration, pyrolysis, and carbonization followed by dearomatization (Scheme 1 ). Glucosides and Glucosinolates are carbohydrate families and linked to other molecules. These molecules actively soluble in water and undergo hydrolysis, degradation, on heating in the presence of acids like ascorbic acid (Bones and Rossiter, 2006). After that, papain flavonoids undergo structural deformation when the temperature is maintained at 180 οC (Chaaban et al., 2017; Sabliov et al., 2009a, 2009b) for 24 h. During this time, the color of the solution changed from green to brown. Then the solution was cooled and filtered. The filtrate was diluted with water, pale-yellow CDs appeared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance and luminescence studies of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs were examined by UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy and UV-lamp. In UV–Visible absorption spectra showed peaks at 270 and 330 nm corresponding to π to π* and n to π* transitions belongs to carbon-carbon double bond and carbonyl (CO) bond respectively (Fig. 1 a). This solution shows fluorescence activity at 365 nm (Fig. 1 Inset) under a UV lamp. The CDs are yellow under daylight, but they exhibited blue fluorescence when the sample is placed under UV light. Photoluminescence, a characteristic of CDs, was studied by gradually increasing the excitation wavelengths from 250 to 650 nm (Fig. 1b). These results were indicating that excitation wavelength-dependent the emission nature of CDs. The maximum emission was observed at 530 nm in at 440 nm excitation in Fig S2a. The crude leaf extract neither exhibited fluorescence (Fig S1) nor wavelength-dependent excitation as CDs (Fig S2b & c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and composition studies of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: From FTIR spectroscopy, the surface functional groups of CDs were identified. The FTIR spectra of CDs indicate O–H functional group presence corresponding to broad absorption bands at 3250 cm−1 (Fig. 2 a) (Tran et al., 2020). A sharp peak at 1650 cm−1 is evident for the presence of the CO functional group (Van Tran et al., 2019).

The size, shape, and particle size distribution of CDs were found by TEM. By analyzing the TEM images, CDs appeared uniformly spherical with excellent visibility (Fig. 2b) and showed mono dispersion. The black spheres' average size was found to be 8 nm post calculation within a distribution range of 0–20 nm (Inset: Fig. 2b).

We examined the synthesized CDs for free radical scavenging activity capacity by the DPPH method. DPPH free radical assay is one of the well-established, reliable, and simple methods for estimating antioxidant activity. The activity is dependent on the ability of antioxidant to donate hydrogen ions. The free radical scavenging capability helps in maintaining proper cellular functioning by minimizing the damage caused due to generation of free radicals. The assay is studied by observing a color transformation from purple DPPH to yellow in presence of antioxidant. Ascorbic acid, a commercial antioxidant, is preferred to compare the antioxidant efficiency of the CDs. Fig. 3 represents the free radical scavenging activity of various concentrations of CDs (0–50 μg/mL) and ascorbic acid. As shown in Fig. 3a, the DPPH characteristic absorption peak at a wavelength of 517 nm gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of CDs is observed. An increase in radical scavenging activity is seen from 59 to 86%, with a gradual color change from purple to yellow (Fig. 3 b & c). It is probably due to the delocalized free radicals in the DPPH accept hydrogen radicals from surface functional groups of CDs to form DPPH2 stable complex. These results also indicate that the free radical scavenging activity of CDs is increasing proportionally with concentration. The EC50 (the minimum inhibitory value) of the CDs was 27.6 μg/μL shown in Fig. 3d. These results confirm that CDs are an alternative significant antioxidant agent.

Phosphomolybdate assay is a quantitative method to estimate the total antioxidant capacity (TAC) of antioxidants (Prieto et al., 1999). TAC is stated in terms of gram equivalents of ascorbic acid. Various CDs concentrations were added to phosphomolybdate reagent to estimate TAC, and the results obtained were shown in Fig (4) . Fig. 4a shows a characteristic increase in the absorption at wavelength 695 nm due to phosphomolybdate V complex formation. In Fig. 4b, with the increase in CDs concentration (0–50 μg/mL), CDs total antioxidant capacity was found to increase from 115 to 315 μg/mL of ascorbic acid. Fig. 4c represents the optical images showing the blue color solution formation after incubation of 90 min. The formation of phosphomolybdate V complex can be explained as the resultant reduction of Molybdenum (VI to V) due to electron-donating groups on CDs surface. Also, CDs can inhibit oxidation, thereby preventing the generation of free radicals and their related disorders. The EC50 was calculated to be 23 μg/mL shown in Fig. 4d. Interestingly, the crude papaya leaf extract did not show any color change after 90 min of incubation, indicating the absence of formation of phosphomolybdate V complex. These results demonstrate that CDs can act as good antioxidants.

The majority of treatments for inflammation by drugs involve the stabilization of the lysosome membrane. The mode of the response of the red blood cell membrane is similar to the lysosomal membrane (Parvin et al., 2015). Thus, the HRBC membrane stabilization method based on induced hypotonicity was studied. The anti-inflammatory activity was examined by adding 0.5 mL HRBC suspension to various concentrations of CDs (0–45 μg/μL). The induced hypotonicity offered by CDs were shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a shows the UV Visible absorption spectra of haemoglobin content in the supernatant recorded at a wavelength of 560 nm. Fig. 5b shows the percentage of inhibition/membrane protection offered by CDs and the standard reference drug diclofenac. The inhibition is found to increase (58–97%) with increased concentration (0–45 μg/μL). The results are considered with the standard reference diclofenac (91–98%) protection activity. Fig. 5c represents the optical images of membrane stabilization offered by CDs. The results show that CDs show the ability to inhibit the lysis of overloaded RBC due to induced hypotonicity. The surface functional groups probably prevent the oxidation of the erythrocyte membrane, thereby inhibiting lysis. The EC50 of the CDs was estimated to be 15.52 μg/mL shown in Fig. 5d.

In summary, we have established a facile, economic, and green approach to prepared CDs by a sand bath assisted method in an aqueous solution using a renewable natural resource, Carica papaya leaves. The CDs showed good water solubility, blue fluorescence under UV lamp, excitation-dependent emission, and nano size. The CDs exhibited characteristic radical scavenging activity and antioxidant activity with efficiency greater than 80%. Moreover, CDs also gave significant results related to the anti-inflammatory activity, showing membrane protection to about 85%. An eco-friendly recyclable approach can be adaptable at a larger scale if procuring and processing are well managed. Indeed, this work results show CDs role in biomedical applications along with easy disposal of biowaste and continue to explore many industrial applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to GITAM, Hyderabad for providing laboratory facilities.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2021.111854.
Krishna kanthi Gudimella: Performed all experiments and involved in manuscript preparation.
Dr. Gangaraju Gedda: He generated the idea, designed all experiments and monitored. He also involved in the Manuscript preparation and revision.
Dr. P. Senthil Kumar, Dr. Deepak Kumar and Dr. Prabal Pratap Singh: All these authors involved in material characterization and scientific discussion.
Dr. BK Babu Dr. Bhaskar Yamajala: Both these authors involved in revising manuscript and scientific discussion.
Dr. Battula Venkateswara Rao: He supervised work and provided valuable suggestion.
Dr. Ajit Sharma: He arranged all experimental data in systematic way.
